{/* Navigation */}
Phylum Arthropoda | Insecta

Honey Bee

Apis mellifera

Social Structure Eusocial
Diet Nectar & Pollen
Role Pollinator

Social insects living in well-organized colonies. Vital for pollination and known for producing honey, wax, and royal jelly.

{ const content = document.querySelector('.container'); if (content) content.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth' }); }} >
{/* Introduction Section [cite: 3] */}

Introduction to Honey Bees

  • Honey bees are social insects that live together in well-organized colonies.
  • Each colony functions as a single unit where every bee has a specific role.
  • They are vital for pollination, helping plants reproduce.
  • They produce honey, wax, royal jelly, and propolis for human use.
{/* Interactive Cards Grid [cite: 8, 9] */}
{/* Queen Card [cite: 10-14] */}
{/* Replace src below with "/public/queenbee.jpeg" */}

Queen Bee

Hover/Tap to Flip

The Queen

  • Only fertile female (Diploid 2n).
  • Main function: Laying eggs.
  • Lays both fertilized & unfertilized eggs.
  • Controls reproduction using the Spermatheca.
{/* Worker Card [cite: 15-24] */}
{/* Replace src below with "/public/workerbee.jpeg" */}

Worker Bee

Hover/Tap to Flip

The Worker

  • Sterile females (Diploid 2n)[cite: 16].
  • Produced from fertilized eggs[cite: 17].
  • Duties: Collecting nectar/pollen, making honey, feeding larvae, cleaning hive .
  • They do not reproduce[cite: 24].
{/* Drone Card [cite: 25-29] */}
{/* Replace src below with "/public/dronebee.jpeg" */}

Drone Bee

Hover/Tap to Flip

The Drone

  • Male bees (Haploid n).
  • Develop from unfertilized eggs.
  • Only function: Mate with the Queen.
  • Dies immediately after mating.
{/* Comparative Table Section */}
Quick Comparison

Colony Roles & Genetics

Type Sex Ploidy Origin Key Role
Queen Female Diploid (2n) Fertilized Egg Reproduction
Worker Female Diploid (2n) Fertilized Egg Work/Foraging
Drone Male Haploid (n) Unfertilized Egg Mating
{/* Reproductive System Section [cite: 30-36] */}
Genetics

Reproductive System

Honey bees exhibit a unique Haplodiploid system of sex determination.The sex of the bee depends entirely on whether the egg is fertilized.

Female Bees (Queen/Worker) Develop from Fertilized Eggs.
They have two sets of chromosomes.
Status: Diploid (2n = 32)
Male Bees (Drones) Develop from Unfertilized Eggs.
They have one set of chromosomes.
Status: Haploid (n = 16)
{/* Parthenogenesis Section */}
Key Mechanism

Parthenogenesis

Parthenogenesis is a form of reproduction where an egg develops into an embryo without fertilization.

  • Common in insects like bees, ants, and wasps.
  • In honey bees, this process produces only Male Drones.
  • Allows the colony to produce males quickly to ensure mating opportunities.
Parthenogenesis in Honey bee
  • Queen lays unfertilized egg intentionally.
  • These develop directly into Drones[cite: 43].
  • Formula: Unfertilized Egg → Male (n).
💡 Memory Tip: Parthenogenesis = Quick Males + Energy Saving + Colony Survival.
{/* Gamified Quiz Section */} {/*

Knowledge Check 🧠

Which bee develops from an unfertilized egg?

*/}
); }; export default HoneyBeePage;

import React from 'react'; import { Target, Dna, Activity, Layers, UserCheck, ShieldCheck, Microscope, Stethoscope } from 'lucide-react'; import { ScrollReveal } from '../../../shared/ScrollReveal'; import './Aschelminthes.css'; // Using the local image path import aschImg from '../../../../assets/images/KingdomAnimalia/aschelminthes.png'; const Aschelminthes = () => { return (
{/* --- HERO SECTION --- */}
Phylum 05 Pseudocoelomate

Aschelminthes

The Roundworms (Asches – cavity; helminth – worm)

Aschelminthes, commonly known as roundworms, are distinguished by their cylindrical bodies that appear circular in cross-section. They represent a significant evolutionary step, possessing a complete digestive system and a pseudocoelom (a false body cavity).

Found in almost every environment on Earth, from deep ocean sediments to fertile soil, many species are also medically significant parasites of plants and animals.

Modern taxonomy often refers to the primary group within this phylum as Nematoda.

MICROSCOPIC ROUNDWORM (NEMATODA)
{/* --- ANATOMY SECTION --- */}

Internal Anatomy & Digestive System

GENERAL ANATOMY OF A MALE NEMATODE

Structural Highlights:

  • Muscular Pharynx: A highly efficient pumping organ that sucks in food.
  • Complete Alimentary Canal: A 'tube-within-a-tube' design with both a mouth and an anus.
  • Syncytial Epidermis: A multi-nucleate layer protected by a thick, non-living cuticle.
  • Renette Cells: Specialized H-shaped cells for the removal of metabolic waste.
{/* --- EXAM FOCUS SECTION --- */}

Exam High-Yield Points

  • Pseudo-coelom: Only phylum with a body cavity not fully lined by mesoderm (mesoderm occurs as scattered pouches).
  • Sexual Dimorphism: Dioecious (sexes are separate). Females are larger than males.
  • Male Anatomy: Males possess a curved tail and Penial Setae (spicules) for copulation.
  • Level of Org: Organ-system level of organization.
  • Body Type: Triploblastic and Bilaterally Symmetrical.
  • Famous Examples: Ascaris (Roundworm), Wuchereria (Filarial worm), Ancylostoma (Hookworm).
{/* --- GENERAL FEATURES GRID --- */}

Core Biological Blueprint

Level of Org. Organ System
Symmetry Bilateral
Germ Layers Triploblastic
Body Cavity Pseudocoelomate
{/* --- MEDICALLY SIGNIFICANT EXAMPLES --- */}

Medical & Veterinary Importance

Ascaris lumbricoides

Common intestinal roundworm of humans. Causes ascariasis.

  • Internal parasite
  • Oral-fecal transmission

Wuchereria bancrofti

The filarial worm that causes Elephantiasis (Lymphatic Filariasis).

  • Transmitted by Culex mosquito
  • Blocks lymph vessels

Ancylostoma duodenale

The human hookworm. Attaches to intestinal walls and sucks blood.

  • Causes anemia
  • Larvae penetrate skin
{/* --- CONCLUSION --- */}
Aschelminthes demonstrate the evolution of the "pseudo-coelom"—a clever engineering solution that provides a hydrostatic skeleton for efficient locomotion.
); }; export default Aschelminthes;

import React from 'react'; import { Activity, Dna, Box, Layers, Orbit, ShieldCheck, Microscope, Sun, CheckCircle2, Sparkles } from 'lucide-react'; import { ScrollReveal } from '../../../shared/ScrollReveal'; import './Ctenophora.css'; const Ctenophora = () => { return (
{/* --- HERO SECTION --- */}
Phylum 03 Bioluminescent

Ctenophora

The Comb Jellies (Kteis – comb; phoros – bearing)

Ctenophores, commonly known as sea walnuts or comb jellies, are exclusively marine, radially symmetrical organisms. They are famed for their breathtaking ability to emit light, turning the dark ocean into a glowing spectacle.

Unlike true jellyfish, they do not possess stinging cells. Instead, they glide gracefully through the water using eight external rows of ciliated comb plates, marking a distinct evolutionary path in the animal kingdom.

Their most striking property is Bioluminescence—a biochemical emission of light that serves as camouflage, startling predators, or luring prey.

REPRESENTATIVE CTENOPHORE (COMB JELLY)
{/* --- ANATOMY & LOCOMOTION SECTION --- */}

Comb Plates & Sticky Cells

INTERNAL ANATOMY OF A CYDIPPID

Unique Mechanisms:

  • Comb Plates (Ctenes): Eight external rows of fused cilia used for swimming. Largest animals to move solely by cilia.
  • Colloblasts: Specialized sticky cells on tentacles (not stinging) used to "glue" onto prey.
  • Aboral Sense Organ: A statocyst at the opposite end of the mouth that coordinates the beating of comb rows.
{/* --- EXAM FOCUS SECTION --- */}

Exam High-Yield Points

  • Exclusively Marine: Found only in saltwater (never freshwater).
  • Locomotion: via 8 ciliated comb plates.
  • Colloblasts: Characteristic sticky cells (also known as Lasso cells).
  • Bioluminescence: Well-marked property of emitting light.
  • Reproduction: Strictly Sexual and hermaphroditic. Fertilization is External.
  • Development: Indirect, via a unique Cydippid larva.
{/* --- GENERAL FEATURES GRID --- */}

Architectural Features

Level of Org. Tissue Level
Symmetry Radial Symmetry
Germ Layers Diploblastic
Digestion Intra & Extracellular
{/* --- CLASSIFICATION --- */}

Major Classes

Tentaculata

Species possessing long, retractable tentacles equipped with sticky colloblasts.

  • Pleurobrachia (Sea gooseberry)
  • Cestum (Venus's girdle)
  • Ctenoplana

Nuda

Species entirely lacking tentacles, capturing prey by engulfing them whole.

  • Beroe
{/* --- CONCLUSION --- */}
Drifting gracefully through the ocean's depths, Ctenophores remain a brilliant testament to nature's ability to innovate complex locomotion and bioluminescence.
); }; export default Ctenophora; phora;

Effects are intended to synchronize state between React and external systems such as manually updating the DOM, state management libraries, or other platform APIs. In general, the body of an effect should do one or both of the following:
* Update external systems with the latest state from React.
* Subscribe for updates from some external system, calling setState in a callback function when external state changes.

import React from 'react'; import { Activity, Dna, Box, Layers, Orbit, ShieldCheck, Microscope, Droplets, CheckCircle2, Scissors } from 'lucide-react'; import { ScrollReveal } from '../../../shared/ScrollReveal'; import './Platyhelminthes.css'; // Using the local image path import platyImg from '../../../../assets/images/KingdomAnimalia/platyhelminthes.png'; const Platyhelminthes = () => { return (
{/* --- HERO SECTION --- */}
Phylum 04 Flatworms

Platyhelminthes

The Dorsiventrally Flattened Worms (Platys – flat; helmin – worm)

Platyhelminthes are the first group of animals to evolve a bilaterally symmetrical, triploblastic body plan. Commonly known as flatworms, their bodies are leaf-like or ribbon-like, ideally suited for their role as either free-living scavengers or specialized endoparasites.

They represent a major evolutionary milestone: the development of cephalization (head formation) and a dedicated excretory system. Whether it's the incredible regenerative powers of Planaria or the complex life cycles of tapeworms, they are masters of adaptation.

Most flatworms are Endoparasites, found in animals including humans, where they possess hooks and suckers for attachment.

FREE-LIVING MARINE FLATWORM
{/* --- ANATOMY & REGENERATION SECTION --- */}

Internal Anatomy & Flame Cells

INTERNAL STRUCTURE OF PLANARIA

Biological Highlights:

  • Flame Cells: Also known as protonephridia. Specialized cells for osmoregulation and excretion.
  • Cephalization: First animals to have a 'head' with concentrated nervous tissue (ganglia) and eyespots.
  • Incomplete Gut: Digestive system has a single opening; branched gastrovascular cavity distributes nutrients.
  • Regeneration: Planaria can regenerate an entire organism from a tiny fragment of its body.
{/* --- EXAM FOCUS SECTION --- */}

Exam High-Yield Points

  • Germ Layers: First Triploblastic phylum (Ecto, Meso, Endoderm).
  • Coelom: Exclusively Acoelomate (no body cavity). Space filled with Parenchyma tissue.
  • Symmetry: First Bilateral Symmetry in the animal kingdom.
  • Excretion: via Flame Cells (Protonephridia). (Very common MCQ point).
  • Heredity: Mostly hermaphrodites (Monoecious). Fertilization is Internal.
  • Parasitic Adaptations: Thick tegument, hooks, and suckers for attachment. Direct absorption of nutrients.
{/* --- GENERAL FEATURES GRID --- */}

Architectural Features

Level of Org. Organ/Organ-System
Symmetry Bilateral Symmetry
Germ Layers Triploblastic
Body Plan Blind-sac Body Plan
{/* --- CLASSIFICATION --- */}

Major Classes

Turbellaria

Mostly free-living, aquatic flatworms with a ciliated epidermis.

  • Planaria (Dugesia)

Trematoda

Endoparasitic 'flukes' with suckers. Usually have complex life cycles with multiple hosts.

  • Fasciola (Liver fluke)
  • Schistosoma (Blood fluke)

Cestoda

Tapeworms. Strictly endoparasitic with a segmented body (proglottids) and no gut.

  • Taenia solium (Pork tapeworm)
  • Taenia saginata (Beef tapeworm)
{/* --- CONCLUSION --- */}
Platyhelminthes pioneered the bilateral triploblastic design, paving the way for the complex organ systems seen in higher animals.
); }; export default Platyhelminthes;

import React from 'react'; import { Activity, Dna, Box, Layers, Orbit, ShieldCheck, Microscope, Droplets, CheckCircle2 } from 'lucide-react'; import { ScrollReveal } from '../../../shared/ScrollReveal'; import './Porifera.css'; const Porifera = () => { return (
{/* --- HERO SECTION --- */}
Phylum 01 Pore Bearers

Porifera

The Aquatic Filter Feeders (Porus – pore; ferre – to bear)

Sponges represent the most primitive group of multicellular animals. They exhibit a unique cellular level of organization with mostly asymmetrical bodies perfectly adapted to a sessile, filter-feeding lifestyle.

Found predominantly in marine environments—though a few inhabit freshwater—they fascinate scientists with their lack of tissues or organs, relying entirely on specialised cells and a complex internal water canal system.

Central to their anatomy are Choanocytes (collar cells), flagellated cells that line the spongocoel and propel water through the body.

REPRESENTATIVE MARINE SPONGE
{/* --- ANATOMY DIAGRAM SECTION --- */}

Cellular Anatomy & Water Flow

BODY STRUCTURE & CELL TYPES

Key Cellular Components:

  • Pinacocytes: Flat cells forming the outer "skin" (pinacoderm).
  • Choanocytes: Collar cells that create water current and trap food.
  • Archaeocytes: Totipotent cells (can become any cell type) involved in digestion and reproduction.
  • Porocytes: Tubular cells that form the Ostia (inlet pores).
{/* --- EXAM FOCUS SECTION --- */}

Exam High-Yield Points

  • Canal System: Unique feature. 3 types: Ascon (simplest), Sycon, and Leucon (most complex).
  • Water Pathway: Ostia → Spongocoel → Osculum.
  • Digestion: Exclusively Intracellular (occurs inside food vacuoles).
  • Skeleton: Made of Spicules (calcereous/siliceous) or Spongin fibres.
  • Reproduction: Asexual by fragmentation or Gemmules (internal buds). Sexual by internal fertilization.
  • Larval Stage: Indirect development. Characteristic larvae are Amphiblastula or Parenchymula.
{/* --- GENERAL FEATURES GRID --- */}

Architectural Features

Level of Org. Cellular Level
Symmetry Asymmetrical
Germ Layers Diploblastic
Coelom Acoelomate
{/* --- CLASSIFICATION --- */}

Major Classes

Calcarea

Calcareous sponges with spicules made of calcium carbonate. Strictly marine.

  • Sycon (Scypha)
  • Leucosolenia

Hexactinellida

Glass sponges featuring a delicate skeleton of six-rayed siliceous spicules.

  • Euplectella (Venus flower basket)

Demospongiae

Largest class. Skeleton of spongin fibres, siliceous spicules, or both.

  • Spongilla (Freshwater sponge)
  • Euspongia (Bath sponge)
{/* --- CONCLUSION --- */}
The simplicity of Porifera masks an incredibly efficient biological design that has remained virtually unchanged and ecologically vital for hundreds of millions of years.
); }; export default Porifera; orifera;

  { name: "homo erectus", scientificName: "Upright man", classKey: "chordata", route: "/zoohub/chordata/homo_erectus", thumbnail: "https://res.cloudinary.com/duibfmcw1/image/upload/v1771228497/Homo_erectus_%EF%B8%8E_tgx2w4.png" },
  { name: "homo neanderthalensis", scientificName: "Neanderthal", classKey: "chordata", route: "/zoohub/chordata/homo_neanderthalensis", thumbnail: "https://res.cloudinary.com/duibfmcw1/image/upload/v1771228516/Homo_neanderthalensis_%EF%B8%8E_quqisx.png" },
  { name: "homo sapiens", scientificName: "Human", classKey: "chordata", route: "/zoohub/chordata/homo_sapiens", thumbnail: "https://res.cloudinary.com/duibfmcw1/image/upload/v1771228525/Homo_sapiens_gwfpme.png" },
];

function ZooHub() {
  const navigate = useNavigate();
  const location = useLocation();

  // Track if sticky bar should be visible
  const [isSticky, setIsSticky] = useState(false);
  const [stickyTop, setStickyTop] = useState(72);
  const bannerRef = useRef(null);



  // 🔗 refs for all phyla
  const refs = {
    porifera: useRef(null),
    coelenterata: useRef(null),
    ctenophora: useRef(null),
    platyhelminthes: useRef(null),
    aschelminthes: useRef(null),
    annelida: useRef(null),
    arthropoda: useRef(null),
    mollusca: useRef(null),
    echinodermata: useRef(null),
    hemichordata: useRef(null),
    chordata: useRef(null),
  };

  // 🔍 search state
  const [query, setQuery] = useState("");
  const [results, setResults] = useState([]);
  const [isSearching, setIsSearching] = useState(false);
  const [isMobile, setIsMobile] = useState(window.innerWidth <= 900);



  // Track scroll to make sticky bar appear after banner & stay attached to header
  useEffect(() => {
    const handleScroll = () => {
      // Use the header's actual bottom position — this naturally handles:
      // - Header at full height (72px)
      // - Header shrunk on scroll (60px)
      // - Header hidden on mobile (bottom = 0 or negative)
      const headerEl = document.querySelector('.hea-header');
      const headerBottom = headerEl ? Math.max(0, headerEl.getBoundingClientRect().bottom) : 72;
      setStickyTop(headerBottom);

      if (bannerRef.current) {
        const bannerBottom = bannerRef.current.getBoundingClientRect().bottom;
        setIsSticky(bannerBottom <= headerBottom);
      }

      setIsMobile(window.innerWidth <= 900);
    };

    window.addEventListener('scroll', handleScroll, { passive: true });
    return () => window.removeEventListener('scroll', handleScroll);
  }, []);

  // 🔗 Auto-scroll based on URL route
  useEffect(() => {
    const pathParts = location.pathname.split('/');
    const phylumFromUrl = pathParts[pathParts.length - 1];

    if (phylumFromUrl && refs[phylumFromUrl]) {
      setTimeout(() => {
        refs[phylumFromUrl]?.current?.scrollIntoView({ behavior: "smooth" });
      }, 300);
    }
  }, [location.pathname]);



  // 🔝 scroll to class
  const scrollToClass = (key) => {
    refs[key]?.current?.scrollIntoView({ behavior: "smooth" });
  };

  // 🔍 species search with delay for skeleton effect
  const handleSearchChange = (e) => {
    const value = e.target.value.toLowerCase();
    setQuery(value);

    if (!value) {
      setResults([]);
      setIsSearching(false);
      return;
    }

    // Show skeleton briefly for perceived performance
    setIsSearching(true);
    setTimeout(() => {
      // Search phyla
      const phylumResults = phylumMap.filter((p) => {
        const nameMatch = p.name.toLowerCase().includes(value);
        const descMatch = (p.description || "").toLowerCase().includes(value);
        return nameMatch || descMatch;
      });

      // Search taxonomic classes
      const classResults = classMap.filter((c) => {
        return c.name.toLowerCase().includes(value);
      });

      // Search species
      const speciesResults = speciesMap.filter((s) => {
        const nameMatch = (s.name || "").toLowerCase().includes(value);
        const phylumMatch = (s.classKey || "").toLowerCase().includes(value);
        const scientificMatch = (s.scientificName || "").toLowerCase().includes(value);
        const slugMatch = (s.slug || "").toLowerCase().includes(value);
        return nameMatch || phylumMatch || scientificMatch || slugMatch;
      });

      // Phyla first, then classes, then species
      setResults([...phylumResults, ...classResults, ...speciesResults]);
      setIsSearching(false);
    }, 200);
  };

  // 🔗 Navigate to species, phylum, or class page
  const handleSpeciesClick = (item) => {
    if (item.type === "phylum" || item.type === "class") {
      // Navigate to ZooHub and scroll to the phylum section
      navigate("/zoohub");
      setTimeout(() => {
        refs[item.scrollKey]?.current?.scrollIntoView({ behavior: "smooth" });
      }, 300);
    } else {
      const path = item.route || `/zoohub/${item.classKey}/${item.slug || item.name.replace(/\s+/g, '-').toLowerCase()}`;
      navigate(path);
    }
    setQuery("");
    setResults([]);
  };

  const handleSearch = () => {
    if (results.length > 0) {
      handleSpeciesClick(results[0]);
    }
  };

  const handleKeyDown = (e) => {
    if (e.key === 'Enter') {
      handleSearch();
    }
  };

  return (
    <div className="zoohub-page">
      {/* 🎯 BANNER - Home page style with left content + right carousel */}
      {/* 🎯 FULL SCREEN HERO BANNER */}
      <div className="zoohub-banner" ref={bannerRef}>
        <div className="zoohub-banner-overlay"></div>
        <div className="zoohub-banner-content">
          <ScrollReveal animation="fade-up" duration={1000}>
            <div className="zoohub-banner-center">
              <img
                src="https://res.cloudinary.com/duibfmcw1/image/upload/v1765947727/logopng_2_webaac.png"
                alt="ZooLearn Logo"
                className="zoohub-banner-logo"
              />
              <h1 className="zoohub-banner-brand">ZooHub</h1>
            </div>
          </ScrollReveal>
        </div>

        {/* 🖱️ SCROLL INDICATOR */}
        <div className="zoohub-scroll-indicator" onClick={() => scrollToClass('porifera')}>
          <div className="mouse">
            <div className="wheel"></div>
          </div>
          <div className="arrow-down"></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div
        className={`zoohub-sticky-bar ${isSticky ? 'is-sticky' : ''}`}
        style={isSticky ? { top: `${stickyTop}px` } : undefined}
      >
        {/* Class Navigation */}
        <div className="class-navbar">
          <div className="class-scroll">
            {Object.keys(phylumIcons).map((key) => (
              <span key={key} onClick={() => scrollToClass(key)}>
                {phylumIcons[key]} {key}
              </span>
            ))}
          </div>
        </div>

        {/* Search Bar */}
        <div className="search-section">
          <div className="search-container">
            <div className="search-input-wrapper">
              <span className="search-icon-text">🔍</span>
              <input
                type="text"
                placeholder={isMobile ? "Search species..." : "Search species or phylum (e.g., hydra, chordata, octopus...)"}
                value={query}
                onChange={handleSearchChange}
                onKeyDown={handleKeyDown}
              />
            </div>
            <button className="search-btn" onClick={handleSearch}>
              Search
            </button>

            {/* Enhanced Search Results with Thumbnails */}
            {query && (isSearching || results.length > 0) && (
              <ul className="search-results">
                {isSearching ? (
                  <>
                    <SkeletonSearchItem />
                    <SkeletonSearchItem />
                    <SkeletonSearchItem />
                  </>
                ) : (
                  results.map((item, i) => (
                    <li key={i} onClick={() => handleSpeciesClick(item)} className="search-result-item">
                      {item.type === "phylum" ? (
                        <>
                          <span className="search-result-phylum-icon">{item.icon}</span>
                          <div className="search-result-info">
                            <span className="search-result-name" style={{ textTransform: 'capitalize' }}>{item.name}</span>
                            <span
                              className="search-result-phylum"
                              style={{ backgroundColor: phylumColors[item.scrollKey] + '20', color: phylumColors[item.scrollKey] }}
                            >
                              📂 Phylum — {item.description}
                            </span>
                          </div>
                        </>
                      ) : item.type === "class" ? (
                        <>
                          <span className="search-result-phylum-icon">{phylumIcons[item.scrollKey]}</span>
                          <div className="search-result-info">
                            <span className="search-result-name" style={{ textTransform: 'capitalize' }}>{item.name}</span>
                            <span
                              className="search-result-phylum"
                              style={{ backgroundColor: phylumColors[item.scrollKey] + '20', color: phylumColors[item.scrollKey] }}
                            >
                              🏷️ Class — {item.phylum}
                            </span>
                          </div>
                        </>
                      ) : (
                        <>
                          <img
                            src={item.thumbnail}
                            alt={item.name}
                            className="search-result-thumbnail"
                            onError={(e) => {
                              e.target.style.display = 'none';
                            }}
                          />
                          <div className="search-result-info">
                            <span className="search-result-name">{item.name}</span>
                            <span
                              className="search-result-phylum"
                              style={{ backgroundColor: phylumColors[item.classKey] + '20', color: phylumColors[item.classKey] }}
                            >
                              {phylumIcons[item.classKey]} {item.classKey}
                            </span>
                          </div>
                        </>
                      )}
                    </li>
                  ))
                )}
              </ul>
            )}
            {query && !isSearching && results.length === 0 && (
              <div className="search-results" style={{ textAlign: 'center', padding: '1.5rem 1rem', color: '#6c757d', fontSize: '0.95rem' }}>
                🔍 No species found for "{query}"
              </div>
            )}
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>

      {/* 📦 PHYLUM COMPONENTS with Scroll Reveal */}
      <ScrollReveal animation="fade-up" duration={600}>
        <section ref={refs.porifera}>
          <Porifera />
        </section>
      </ScrollReveal>

      <ScrollReveal animation="fade-up" duration={600}>
        <section ref={refs.coelenterata}>
          <Coelenterata />
        </section>
      </ScrollReveal>

      <ScrollReveal animation="fade-up" duration={600}>
        <section ref={refs.ctenophora}>
          <Ctenophora />
        </section>
      </ScrollReveal>

      <ScrollReveal animation="fade-up" duration={600}>
        <section ref={refs.platyhelminthes}>
          <Platyhelminthes />
        </section>
      </ScrollReveal>

      <ScrollReveal animation="fade-up" duration={600}>
        <section ref={refs.aschelminthes}>
          <Aschelminthes />
        </section>
      </ScrollReveal>

      <ScrollReveal animation="fade-up" duration={600}>
        <section ref={refs.annelida}>
          <Annelida />
        </section>
      </ScrollReveal>

      <ScrollReveal animation="fade-up" duration={600}>
        <section ref={refs.arthropoda}>
          <Arthropoda />
        </section>
      </ScrollReveal>

      <ScrollReveal animation="fade-up" duration={600}>
        <section ref={refs.mollusca}>
          <Mollusca />
        </section>
      </ScrollReveal>

      <ScrollReveal animation="fade-up" duration={600}>
        <section ref={refs.echinodermata}>
          <Echinodermata />
        </section>
      </ScrollReveal>

      <ScrollReveal animation="fade-up" duration={600} rootMargin="0px" threshold={0.05}>
        <section ref={refs.hemichordata}>
          <Hemichordata />
        </section>
      </ScrollReveal>

      <ScrollReveal animation="fade-up" duration={600} rootMargin="0px" threshold={0.05}>
        <section ref={refs.chordata}>
          <Chordata />
        </section>
      </ScrollReveal>

      {/* 🔸 Remaining phyla - components to be added later */}
    </div>
  );
}

export default ZooHub;
